content:
If you’ve spent any amount of time building desktop applications with Python, you’ve probably noticed a pattern.
Most of them look like they were designed fifteen years ago.
Gray buttons. Tiny fonts. Awkward layouts. Windows that refuse to resize correctly. Interfaces that technically work but somehow make users want to close them five seconds after opening them.
For a long time, I assumed this was simply the price of using Python.
After all, Python dominates automation, machine learning, scripting, data science, and backend development — not desktop software.
I couldn’t have been more wrong.
The problem wasn’t Python.
The problem was how I — and many other developers — approached GUI development.
Instead of asking:
Which framework makes beautiful desktop applications?
I was asking:
Which framework lets me create a window the fastest?
